physicalDetails.detail: Young human prince in polished plate that stays bright even in Strahnbrad's mud, golden hair damp at the temples, mouth hardened by command and lack of sleep, and Light's Vengeance resting across one forearm with practiced ease.
He looks older than Jaina remembers — still handsome, still easy in his own charisma, but narrowed by fatigue into something harder and less open than memory wants him to be.

Chapter 11 places him over a trestle table pinned with daggers and dispatch tubes, the old princely charisma cut harder by command until even Sylvanas reads him as a man shaped more by certainty than wisdom.
Chapter 15 puts fresh damage on him: a cracked left shoulder plate strapped down, dried blood black in the vambrace scrollwork, and a face wiped clean only as far as utility requires.

At the table he looks less like a prince receiving warning than a commander already leaning into the next city, one gauntlet planted beside Stratholme as if the map can be forced to yield through pressure alone.

Chapter 16 brings him to the gates still road-hot: lather drying on the horse, dust over older armor damage, and then one terrifying blankness in the face once Jaina says the city cannot be saved cleanly.
At Stratholme’s gate he arrives road-dusted and all edge, the princely face emptied of heat the instant Jaina says no clean rescue remains.
During the Culling he appears only in flashes of white-blond hair, plate shoulder, and mounted command through smoke, more function than man.
When he returns under the arch, his gauntlets are off and his hands are washed clean to the wrists, with eyes that read not fevered but emptied out and wrong.

Chapter 18 keeps Arthas physically absent but visually inescapable: pale clean hands in firelight, the remembered shape of command in motion, and then the off-page fact of ships taken north with only his proven men aboard.
Chapter 19 reduces him to strategic gravity more than body: the absent prince whose movement north bends everyone else's map toward the coast.

Chapter 20 keeps him physically absent but renders him through aftermath: enlarged docks, stripped harbor function, and a lead measured in weeks rather than hours, turning his body into missing distance and infrastructure damage.
Chapter 21 puts him back on the page transformed by Frostmourne: thinner, colder, rime spreading from the blade over gauntlet and wrist, face still recognizably his but emptied of hesitation.

Chapter 22 renders Arthas through public aftermath rather than direct body: painted cloth over city gates, smiling stick-figure celebrations, and a palace already reorganizing itself around the prince who came home with Frostmourne and made the kingdom mistake corruption for triumph.

Chapter 23 shows Arthas in the Imperial Chamber as prince-shape hollowed by Frostmourne: dull blond hair, plate rimed at the joints, wounds sealing over until the blade’s rhythm is torn open from within.
When the seam finally holds and Sylvanas’s last arrow lands, he drops like something cut loose from the force that had been carrying him.
Chapter 24 leaves Arthas on page only as aftermath and testimony: dead prince, broken vessel, and the body around which everyone else argues what happened and what the blade made of him.

voiceMarkers.detail: Publicly courteous and almost effortlessly charming, opening with social ease even in a wet war camp.
Listens hard enough to make disbelief sound reasonable; his refusal arrives wrapped in patience, hierarchy, and a tone that keeps trying to sound fair while closing every door.
When challenged too directly, the courtesy shows its teeth — quiet, controlled, and more dangerous for never needing to raise his voice.
Chapter 11 gives him briefing-room velocity: sharp clean questions, fast pattern recognition, and orders that begin before the intelligence has fully stopped being spoken.
His chapter-15 voice is report-hungry and impatience-polished. He interrupts for count, route, and timing, stripping every sentence toward what moves a column faster.
The public courtesy remains, but it now lands blade-first: “Lady Proudmoore” is not warmth, only distance with history still inside it.

His chapter-16 voice has narrowed to command interrogation: well, then we still have a chance, how many districts, can the city still be saved. He strips Jaina's field report down to the single answer he thinks command can still use.
Chapter 17 flattens his voice instead of raising it. The calm is what makes the order carry: level enough to travel farther than a shout and colder for never sounding out of control.
He answers moral resistance with curt finality — “They’re dead already,” “Then stand aside,” “I didn’t ask” — as if contradiction is only delay in another uniform.
His command voice to Falric is stripped to execution language, not persuasion or rallying speech; the city is treated as a task already underway.
Chapter 19 leaves him voiceless; what remains is command residue, the prince as direction rather than speaker.
Chapter 20 keeps him voiceless. His 'voice' is logistical residue now — the scale of harbor expansion and the hard fact that he pushed inland without leaving any easy point of interception behind.
Chapter 21 removes what warmth remained. His speech is flat, certain, and stripped of persuasion: a man announcing completion, not debating consequence.

No live voice on page in chapter22. His presence now speaks through civic celebration, resumed audiences, and a palace confidence so complete it no longer needs him physically present in the scene to dominate it.
His chapter-23 voice is level, cold, and almost conversational in the middle of patricide and combat, which makes the absence of warmth more disturbing than overt fury would have been.

beliefs.belief: A visible civilian crisis in front of him deserves priority over a harder-to-prove invisible one moving through grain and rumor.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and unchallenged inside the chapter; the prose treats it as understandable but dangerously incomplete.

beliefs.belief: Command cannot be rerouted on the strength of one argument without cleaner proof and clearer naming than Jaina can yet provide.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active at chapter end; he partially credits the warning but still refuses major action.

beliefs.belief: Panic can kill as efficiently as plague if a warning is spread carelessly through a still-working city.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as part of his governing instinct, though the chapter frames waiting for permission as its own danger.

beliefs.belief: Once the grain pattern clicks, moving immediately is holier than waiting for the last missing layer of explanation.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed as his active chapter-11 stance and presented as both effective and dangerous.

beliefs.belief: Once Hearthglen starts failing again, speed toward the next city matters more than consolidating the line already under him.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active in chapter 14 and framed as both understandable and dangerous.

beliefs.belief: The larger war can wait its turn; Stratholme is the next decision that matters.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active and unchallenged in chapter 15, though the chapter frames it as part of the danger Uther already sees in him.

beliefs.belief: A useful side attempt may ride ahead, but the main body must not slow for uncertainty.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 15 by his willingness to give Jaina a lane while refusing to halt the larger march.

beliefs.belief: A city-scale crisis must still yield a clean yes-or-no answer fast enough for command, even when the truth is messier than the question.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 16 by the way he keeps forcing Jaina toward the single verdict he can act on.

beliefs.belief: If a city is already seeded, killing the living in controlled sections is preferable to letting the dead claim it all at once.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Active in chapter 17. The prose makes the logic tactically legible while refusing to redeem the choice morally.

beliefs.belief: Once he has the answer he needs, dissent becomes obstruction rather than counsel.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter 17 by the speed with which he sheds both Uther and Jaina from the command chain.

beliefs.belief: The true answer now lies farther north, beyond ordinary Lordaeron response.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Inferred from chapter 18's ship-taking and reinforced by chapter 19's full strategic reorientation around his movement.

beliefs.belief: Speed north matters more than holding a clean supply line or leaving any workable trace for those who follow.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter20 by the weeks-old lead and the built-out Daggercap landing that still cannot be used to catch him quickly.

beliefs.belief: Frostmourne is the answer that justifies every prior cost and will finish the campaign no matter what must be abandoned to claim it.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed in chapter21 by the state in which Arthas is found and by his own refusal of every warning inside the cave.

beliefs.belief: He can occupy Capital City openly and let ceremony, routine, and public gratitude do the work of normalizing what he has become.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Supported in chapter22 by the banners, public joy, resumed audiences, unchanged gate posture, and interior Northrend guard rotation.

beliefs.belief: Frostmourne’s power makes ordinary intrusion irrelevant, whether the threat comes from old love, old loyalty, or battlefield skill.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Disproved in chapter23 once Jaina and Sylvanas force open the seam and make ordinary trajectory matter again.

beliefs.belief: Posthumously, chapter24 confirms that his last governing mistake was treating the blade as final authority rather than as the thing hollowing him out.
beliefs.trueOrFalse: Confirmed by the tribunal evidence and by Jaina's insistence that the weapon remains active after his death.

goalsAtChapterEnd.active: None. Arthas is dead on the throne steps before the chapter closes.
goalsAtChapterEnd.underlying: His final active aim in the room was to complete the succession, destroy intruders, and keep Frostmourne’s advantage intact.

emotionalStateAtChapterEnd.state: Dead. In the last visible instant before death, shock and pain crack through the cold certainty as the sword’s bond tears open.

decisionsThisChapter.detail: Granted Jaina and Sylvanas an audience inside his command space instead of dismissing them outright from the saddle.
Heard the plague case through to actionable requests, then refused to divert major force toward Andorhal while Blackrock raiders were still taking civilians.
Issued only a limited follow-up order to quartermasters about trouble from Andorhal's grain yards, making the warning secondary to the war already in front of him.
Accepts Sylvanas’s plague briefing as actionable truth, immediately ordering local granaries checked, suspect stores burned, and the quartermaster line sealed.
Launches the mounted response before the full analytical foundation is complete, turning the warning into cavalry tempo within the hour.
Invites Sylvanas to ride with him, then lets her refuse without contest once his own line of advance is set.
Does not remain at Hearthglen after the town nearly fails again after dark.
Takes the main body south-east at speed toward Stratholme.
Takes Sylvanas’s full plague-road briefing as command truth and immediately reframes the map around Stratholme.
Lets Jaina and Cyndia ride ahead with route packet, signal priority, and a cleared lane instead of forbidding the attempt outright.
Leaves Sylvanas free to choose between his column and Jaina’s lane, then accepts her choice without argument.
Demands that if the city is already lost, the answer be brought back plain.
Reaches Stratholme with the main column still in usable order and forces Jaina to give him the clean answer at the gate instead of inside speculation.
Keeps pressing Jaina's report toward a yes-or-no even after the warehouse successes prove the truth is more complicated than that.
Orders the gates sealed once his column is through so no one leaves without his word.
Publicly announces the Culling and frames it as a street-by-street, house-by-house military necessity.
Rejects Uther’s refusal and Jaina’s refusal alike, then gives Falric direct instructions to take the first companies through Market Row and burn resistance.
Continues the purge district by district until the city falls quiet under ordered fire and rotation.
Returns beneath the gate only after washing, presenting himself to the wall witnesses in a deliberately cleaner state than the city he has just destroyed.
Commandeers ships on the northern coast and turns away from home and Capital City toward Northrend instead.
Takes with him the men who stayed through Stratholme, selecting for obedience to his worst decision rather than distancing himself from it.
Remains off-page in chapter 19 but still decides the chapter: his northbound movement is the fact that forces Sylvanas and Jaina to abandon the southern line and turn for the coast.
Though still off-page in chapter20, he has already chosen a landing point, expanded the docks there, and driven inland fast enough that the pursuit arrives weeks behind him.
Claims Frostmourne before Sylvanas’s formation arrives, abandons Light’s Vengeance outside the cave, and answers Muradin, Jaina, and Sylvanas as if the choice is already complete.
Uses Frostmourne’s field and the cave itself as weapons, then allows the retreat instead of needing to pursue it.
Has already returned to Capital City, accepted public triumph, resumed audiences, and installed Northrend men on interior rotation by the time the pursuit reaches Lordaeron again.
Murders Terenas in the throne room before the hidden rescue can reach the dais.
Turns immediately from father to intruders, prioritizing the chamber fight and the threatened sword over ceremony.
Repeatedly redirects his attention toward Jaina the moment her work on the seam becomes the greater danger to Frostmourne.

abilitiesDemonstrated.ability: Turns a wet field camp into a personal command center by sheer presence; the space bends around him before he says a word.
Balances princely charm and battlefield authority, shifting between greeting, command, and warning without visible strain.
Holds to a chosen priority under emotional pressure, even when Jaina's knowledge and their shared history both press on the decision.
Converts a map full of partial plague data into immediate military action the moment the frame becomes legible to him.
Turns a large field camp inside out quickly, translating personal conviction into mounted mobilization with almost no lag.
Can turn a provisional hold into renewed offensive movement almost immediately, keeping an army in motion even while the field is still unstable behind him.
Can absorb a complex briefing and turn it into movement orders almost before the last clause is finished.
Keeps a command tent and supply chain moving under exhaustion, damage, and moral disagreement.
Can reduce a city-scale disaster briefing to the one verdict he wants from it and hold the whole gate space still until he gets that answer.
Can convert a citywide atrocity into disciplined urban doctrine, sequencing troops, fire, reserves, and cleared lanes instead of letting the action degrade into riot.
Can hold command cohesion even through a public moral split with both mentor and former lover, keeping his officers and soldiers moving at his chosen pace.
Exerts strategic gravity even in absence; his choice of direction is enough to pull mages, rangers, and support lines after him.
Can turn a seized harbor into a functioning forward base and keep a pursuing force behind him even after the sea crossing is complete.
Survives ordinary lethal shots and direct spell pressure as if his body and the blade now answer to different rules than a living prince’s.
Combines Frostmourne’s field with overwhelming speed and environmental violence, turning the cave itself into part of his attack pattern.
Exerts strategic and political gravity in absence, reorganizing a kingdom around his return so thoroughly that routine itself becomes cover.
Can survive throat and chest wounds and continue fighting so long as Frostmourne’s local rhythm remains intact.
Can project cold shock and black-red force through Frostmourne strongly enough to crack stone and throw trained opponents across the chamber.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Jaina Proudmoore
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses 'Arthas' with no title, letting private history override protocol.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Sylvanas Windrunner
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Reduces him to 'the prince' once the exchange becomes a command problem instead of a personal one.

howOthersAddressHim.speaker: Captain Falric
howOthersAddressHim.pattern: Uses “my prince” in immediate obedience, confirming that the command edge around Arthas is still personal as well as hierarchical.

howHeAddressesOthers.pattern: Begins with 'Lady Proudmoore' in polished public courtesy, then uses 'Jaina' softly when trying to make refusal sound reasonable.
Uses the formal 'Ranger-General,' acknowledging rank without pretending intimacy.
Uses Falric’s name as a release trigger for action; by chapter 17 the captain is less confidant than execution hinge.

howOthersAddressHer.speaker: Villagers and public signage
howOthersAddressHer.pattern: Prince Arthas and hero-language dominate the chapter’s civic voice, proving the propaganda victory has already landed.

bodyLanguagePatterns.detail: Makes a center of himself without obvious effort; command space organizes around his body before the dialogue does.
His shoulders tighten almost imperceptibly at the word plague, and he rests the hammer's head in the mud when choosing the stance that lets him refuse without retreating.
When he issues the small quartermaster concession, he does it without looking away from Jaina, as if even the gesture must remain under his control.

Chapter 11 makes his body language the story of acceleration: hand flattening over Hearthglen on the map, grip whitening on the table edge when challenged, and the whole tent changing temperature once he starts issuing orders.
Tracks roads and districts with his fingers as if route pressure is the only language left that still feels honest to him.
Once the decision lands, he turns away and starts issuing stacked orders without waiting for the room to recover, making motion itself his answer to argument.
At the gate he comes toward Jaina like a man who has carried one question in his teeth for half the road, and the chapter ends with his face emptied rather than calmed once the answer arrives.
His face empties rather than flares when Jaina answers, making blankness itself the warning sign.
Turns away from Uther and Jaina without losing pace, as if shedding them is only one more movement inside the larger operation.
Looks straight up at the wall after the purge, holding the witness line long enough to make the pause feel chosen.

Chapter 21 makes stillness itself wrong around him: pulling an arrow free without hurry, stopping ten paces away to watch the formation fail, and declining pursuit because the outcome is already certain to him.
Chapter 22 gives no direct body, only the bodily imprint of his occupation: relaxed guards, palace routine, and celebratory iconography stretched over the city like a second skin.

knowledge.detail: Knows Strahnbrad is under active Blackrock pressure and that civilians are still being taken or threatened in the immediate theater around him.
Hears that the plague is moving through grain toward Andorhal and that Jaina and Sylvanas believe the road itself is part of the war.
Accepts that there may be trouble in Andorhal's grain system, but not at a level he considers sufficient for immediate large-scale redeployment.

By chapter 9 Arthas matters to the surviving field team less as the man who already refused once and more as the outside command lever still capable of moving faster than committee process if reached in time.

Learns the plague is moving through ordinary grain routes, that Hearthglen and likely Stratholme sit on the same compromised map, and that some of his own camp sickness may already belong to the same system.
Hears Uther’s warning in real time and ignores its braking function, choosing motion over reflection once the target locks.
Now has field confirmation from the relay line that Hearthglen nearly failed again after dark and that the wounded are being burned before kin finish the words over them.
By chapter 14 his movement has made Stratholme the live crisis point rather than a future destination.
Now has the full field picture: Andorhal as throat, grain as shell, bodies as the real engine, Kel’Thuzad dead but the dead still rising, and Stratholme already on the live route.
Understands that Jaina’s only plausible countermeasure is source-side and time-sensitive, aimed at stock before it vanishes into people.
Now knows from Jaina's field report that the source plan works at node scale but cannot pull the plague back out of ovens, stomachs, and blood once the city has already eaten.
Has heard the plain answer he demanded: Stratholme cannot be saved cleanly now.
Now knows beyond dispute that Jaina’s source-side method was real and still too late at city scale.
Knows Uther and Jaina have both refused him in public and proceeds anyway, which means he understands the moral line and chooses to cross it knowingly.
Chapter 18 confirms the next destination of that certainty: not Capital City, not reckoning, but Northrend, with the men most proven in compliance already under him.
By chapter 19 the surviving protagonists know he has ships, a filtered force, and a northerly lead large enough to force every remaining practical question onto the harbor road.
By chapter20 the pursuit knows he landed at Daggercap Bay weeks earlier, expanded the docks, and moved inland fast enough that catching him at the shoreline is no longer possible.
By chapter21 the pursuit knows he has already claimed Frostmourne, discarded Light’s Vengeance, and become something no longer legible as a prince who can be reached by ordinary means.
By chapter22 the protagonists know Arthas has been in Capital City for four days, with extra loyal guards inside the palace and no public interruption strong enough to touch him.
Understands the throne room has been infiltrated and recognizes the true threat as the attack on Frostmourne rather than on his body alone.
In chapter24 Arthas functions as known evidence: his death does not end Frostmourne, and his body becomes proof that destroying the wielder did not destroy the problem.

arc.lie: A threat that cannot yet be struck cleanly can wait behind the one already burning in daylight.
arc.truth: The invisible war in supply, bodies, and timing cannot be beaten by speed alone, no matter how righteous the speed feels.
arc.currentPosition: Chapter 24 keeps Arthas narratively active only as wreckage. His arc as a living actor is over, but the chapter proves the damage he enabled survives him and continues to govern everyone else's choices.

arcHistory.currentPosition: Chapter 6 introduces Arthas as still heroic-looking, still publicly reasonable, and already trapped in the command habit that will make him late to the plague. The chapter does not villainize him into pettiness; it shows the fatal shape of his priorities.
Chapter 11 inverts the earlier refusal without healing it. Arthas now recognizes the right enemy quickly, but the same absolutist command instinct simply flips from delay into dangerous over-speed.

Chapter 14 intensifies the chapter-11 pattern instead of correcting it. Arthas is still moving on the right threat, but he is doing it with the same catastrophic appetite for speed that keeps turning insight into momentum before anyone can price the cost.
Chapter 15 radicalizes the chapter-14 pattern. Arthas is no longer merely fast toward the right target; he now turns every new layer of truth into a quicker collision with Stratholme.

Chapter 16 puts Arthas at the gate where truth can no longer be translated into hope. He finally gets the plain answer he demanded, and the chapter leaves him in the instant where hearing it does not broaden him; it hardens him.

Chapter 17 is the irreversible crossing. Arthas no longer merely hardens under truth; he turns truth into mass killing, breaks publicly with every remaining restraint, and returns from the city washed clean enough to make the choice feel finished inside him.

Chapter 18 carries Arthas offstage but not away. The Culling is no longer only an atrocity; it has become a filter. He now heads north with the men who proved they would follow him through it, which makes Stratholme the selection event for whatever comes next.
Chapter 20 pushes Arthas farther into absence-as-force. He no longer needs on-page presence to dominate the story; the harbor scars and impossible lead make his momentum the chapter's governing fact.
Chapter 21 ends Arthas’s last usable ambiguity. He is no longer merely the prince who moved too fast or chose too hard. He is Frostmourne’s wielder, back on the page and less reachable than ever.

Chapter 22 carries Arthas one step deeper into institutional corruption. He is no longer only the blade’s wielder returning home; he is the prince already installed inside ceremony, audience, and palace routine.

goalsHistory.active: Contain the Blackrock crisis around Strahnbrad while folding Andorhal grain trouble into the command queue rather than the center of operations.
goalsHistory.underlying: Hold military order around the threat he can strike today and avoid tearing apart his current response on evidence he judges incomplete.

goalsHistory.active: Drive the mounted column south toward Hearthglen and the infected grain line before the plague can outrun visible force again.
goalsHistory.underlying: Save Lordaeron by striking the threat at once, trusting decisiveness to compensate for what he still does not understand.

goalsHistory.active: Drive the main body south-east toward Stratholme without ceding tempo.
goalsHistory.underlying: Beat the plague by reaching the next fire before delay turns one failing city into two.

goalsHistory.active: Drive the main column on Stratholme without yielding tempo to uncertainty.
goalsHistory.underlying: Reach the city in time to impose an answer before the plague’s next escalation makes command irrelevant.

goalsHistory.active: Act on Jaina's verdict that Stratholme cannot be saved cleanly.
goalsHistory.underlying: Force the city into an answer command can still impose before plague time and civilian time fully diverge.

goalsHistory.active: Finish the purge and move on to the next necessity without surrendering command momentum.
goalsHistory.underlying: Deny the plague a full citywide rising by forcing the city into sections he can still control.

goalsHistory.active: Drive north to Northrend with a force already sorted by obedience at Stratholme.
goalsHistory.underlying: Pursue the next answer with the same narrowed certainty that made the Culling possible.

goalsHistory.active: Continue inland from Daggercap Bay with a lead already too large for straightforward pursuit.
goalsHistory.underlying: Reach the northern answer before anyone following from Lordaeron can meaningfully close the gap.

goalsHistory.active: Leave the cave with Frostmourne uncontested and continue the campaign as its wielder.
goalsHistory.underlying: Submit fully to the blade’s promised answer rather than to any remaining human appeal, warning, or bond.

goalsHistory.active: Hold the palace and continue presenting himself to Lordaeron as its returned victor.
goalsHistory.underlying: Finish sealing Frostmourne’s corruption into state ceremony and interior control before any meaningful witness can reach the room.

emotionalStateHistory.state: Tired, fixed, and armored behind courtesy — not ignorant of the warning, simply committed to a different priority and settled into that commitment with his full weight.
Charged by certainty and already half gone down the road before anyone has finished naming the cost of his certainty.
Off-page but clearly overdriven — chapter 14 leaves him ahead of the survivors, ahead of consolidation, and already pulling the war toward its next irreversible decision.
Overdriven, fixed, and sharpened by urgency into something almost narrower than thought. He is still hearing people, but speed is now the shape of every answer he can bear.

Blanked by the answer and already hardening around it. Chapter 16 leaves him at the most dangerous point yet: the moment when disappointment, urgency, and certainty stop competing and collapse into one line of will.

Emptied out, colder than fury, and past the need to justify himself. By chapter end he reads less like a prince under strain than like a man who has made room inside himself for atrocity and already moved beyond it.
Off-page, unreachable, and legible only by the direction he has chosen for the men still with him.
Off-page and unreadable except as damage and momentum. Chapter 20 leaves him legible only through the scale of what he has already altered and how far ahead he remains.
Cold, emptied, and past argument. Chapter 21 leaves him readable not as a desperate prince but as Frostmourne’s chosen wielder, calm in a way that feels worse than rage.
Off-page and unreadable except as confidence translated into routine, celebration, and the total absence of visible fear inside his own capital.
